name: SK Rapid Wien
content:
SK Rapid Wien is one of the most historic and successful football clubs in Austria, based in Vienna and founded in 1899. Originally established as the First Viennese Workers’ Football Club, it was renamed Rapid shortly after, reflecting its working-class roots. The club plays its home matches at the Allianz Stadion and is known for its green and white colors, as well as its large and passionate fanbase.

Rapid Wien quickly became a dominant force in Austrian football, winning the first national championship in 1911–12 and going on to claim a record 32 league titles. During the interwar years, the club was a major power in Central European football, and it continued to succeed even during the period of Nazi annexation, when it competed in the Gauliga Ostmark and won the German championship in 1941. The post-war era, particularly from the late 1940s to the 1960s, is often considered one of the most successful periods in the club’s history.

On the international stage, Rapid Wien has also achieved notable success, reaching the final of the UEFA Cup Winners’ Cup twice, in 1985 and 1996. Although the club has been less dominant domestically in recent decades, it remains a key figure in Austrian football and regularly competes in European competitions. Rapid is also famous for its traditions, especially the “Rapidviertelstunde,” where fans passionately support the team in the final 15 minutes of matches, symbolizing the club’s fighting spirit.

name: FK Austria Wien
content:
FK Austria Wien is one of the most successful and traditional football clubs in Austria, based in Vienna and founded in 1911. The club originated from a split within the Vienna Cricket and Football Club and was initially known as Wiener Amateur-SV before adopting its current name in 1926. Austria Wien is historically associated with the middle class and has built a strong identity, playing its home matches at the Generali Arena.

The club enjoyed significant success in the interwar period, winning national titles and achieving international recognition by lifting the Mitropa Cup twice in the 1930s. Legendary forward Matthias Sindelar became the symbol of this golden era. During the Nazi annexation, the club competed in the Gauliga Ostmark, although its activities were affected by political pressures and the persecution of Jewish members. After World War II, Austria Wien re-established itself as a dominant force in domestic football.

In the modern era, Austria Wien has remained one of the leading clubs in Austrian football, winning numerous league titles and Austrian Cups. The team experienced particularly strong periods in the 1970s–1980s and early 1990s, while also achieving success in European competitions, including reaching the UEFA Cup Winners’ Cup final in 1978. Although recent years have seen increased competition, especially from clubs like Red Bull Salzburg, Austria Wien continues to play a key role in Austrian football history and development.

name: FC Red Bull Salzburg
content:
FC Red Bull Salzburg is a professional football club based in Salzburg, Austria, originally founded in 1933 as SV Austria Salzburg. The club was formed through a merger of local teams and spent decades under various names due to sponsorship deals, including SV Casino Salzburg and SV Wüstenrot Salzburg. Its first major success came in the 1990s, when it won the Austrian Bundesliga title in 1994 and reached the UEFA Cup final in the same year, marking its emergence on the European stage.

A major turning point in the club’s history came in 2005, when the company Red Bull GmbH acquired and rebranded the team. The club’s name, crest, and traditional violet colors were changed to reflect the Red Bull identity, which caused significant controversy among supporters and led to the creation of a new club by fans who wanted to preserve the original heritage. Despite the backlash, the investment transformed Salzburg into a modern, highly organized football institution with strong financial backing.

Since the takeover, Salzburg has become the dominant force in Austrian football, winning numerous league titles and domestic cups, including long winning streaks in the Bundesliga. The club is also known for its focus on developing young talent, with players like Sadio Mané and Erling Haaland passing through before moving to top European clubs. On the international stage, Salzburg has been a regular participant in UEFA competitions, reaching the Europa League semi-finals in 2018 and consistently appearing in the Champions League group stages, further establishing its reputation in European football.

name: FC Wacker Innsbruck
content:
FC Wacker Innsbruck was a historic football club from Innsbruck, founded in 1915, and became one of the most important teams in Austrian football, particularly during the 20th century. The club initially played at a regional level before reaching the top division in 1964. Its golden era began in the late 1960s and 1970s, when Wacker established itself as a dominant force, winning multiple Austrian championships and domestic cups, and regularly competing in European competitions.

A major turning point came in 1971 when the club merged with another Tyrolean side to form a stronger regional team, which went on to win several league titles and achieve notable success in Europe, including reaching the quarter-finals of the European Cup in 1977–78. Over the following decades, however, the club experienced instability, including relegations, name changes, and the transfer of its Bundesliga license to successor clubs such as FC Tirol Innsbruck. Despite occasional returns to the top division, Wacker struggled to maintain long-term stability.

By the 1990s, financial and structural problems had significantly weakened the club, and after losing its top-flight status and dropping down the league system, FC Wacker Innsbruck was eventually dissolved in 1999. Its legacy continued through successor and phoenix clubs, including a re-founded version in 2002 that sought to revive the traditions of the original team. Despite its turbulent history, Wacker Innsbruck remains an important part of Austrian football heritage, especially in the Tyrol region.

name: FC Swarovski Tirol
content:
FC Swarovski Tirol was an Austrian football club based in Innsbruck, founded in 1986 and dissolved in 1992. The club was created by the crystal company Swarovski as a split from FC Wacker Innsbruck, taking over its Bundesliga license and much of its squad. This allowed the new team to immediately compete at the highest level of Austrian football, quickly establishing itself as a strong force in the league.

Despite its short existence, the club achieved notable success, particularly under coach Ernst Happel. FC Swarovski Tirol won back-to-back Austrian championships in 1989 and 1990 and also lifted the Austrian Cup in 1989. The team was competitive in European competitions as well, reaching the semi-finals of the UEFA Cup in the 1986–87 season, which remains one of the best international performances by an Austrian club.

However, the club’s existence was relatively brief due to structural and financial decisions. In 1992, FC Swarovski Tirol was dissolved, and its Bundesliga license was returned to Wacker Innsbruck before later passing to FC Tirol Innsbruck, considered its successor. Although it lasted only six years, the club left a significant mark on Austrian football through its domestic titles and strong European campaigns.

name: FC Tirol Innsbruck
content:
FC Tirol Innsbruck was an Austrian football club based in Innsbruck, founded in 1993 and dissolved in 2002. The club emerged as a continuation of earlier Tyrolean football structures, taking over the Bundesliga license from FC Wacker Innsbruck after a series of reorganizations that also involved FC Swarovski Tirol. Initially known as FC Innsbruck Tirol, the club was able to compete immediately at the top level of Austrian football due to this licensing structure.

The club reached its peak success at the turn of the millennium, winning three consecutive Austrian Bundesliga titles in 2000, 2001, and 2002. Under coach Kurt Jara, FC Tirol Innsbruck became the dominant force in Austrian football during this period. The team also competed regularly in European competitions, including UEFA Champions League qualifiers and the UEFA Cup, achieving respectable results against notable European clubs.

Despite its sporting success, the club faced severe financial problems. Shortly after winning the 2001–02 championship, FC Tirol Innsbruck declared bankruptcy and lost its Bundesliga license, leading to its dissolution in 2002. A phoenix club, FC Wacker Innsbruck (2002), was later established to continue football traditions in the region.

name: Admira Wacker
content:
Admira Wacker is a professional football club based in Maria Enzersdorf, Austria, with roots that trace back to two historic Vienna clubs: SK Admira (founded in 1905) and SC Wacker (founded in 1907). The modern club was formed in 1971 through the merger of these two sides, combining their traditions and successes. Admira was particularly dominant in the interwar period, winning multiple Austrian championships and cups, while Wacker also enjoyed success, especially in the 1940s, including a league and cup double in 1947.

During the 20th century, both predecessor clubs played important roles in Austrian football. SK Admira was one of the strongest teams of its era, regularly competing at the top level and even reaching the final of the Mitropa Cup in 1934. During the Nazi period, the club participated in the Gauliga Ostmark and reached the final of the German championship in 1939. After World War II, both Admira and Wacker experienced fluctuations in performance and financial stability, which ultimately led to their merger as a way to remain competitive.

Since becoming Admira/Wacker, the club has had periods in both the top and lower divisions of Austrian football. One of its notable achievements in the modern era was reaching the quarter-finals of the European Cup Winners’ Cup in 1990. The club has also undergone further structural changes, including a merger with VfB Mödling in 1997 and several financial restructurings. Despite challenges, Admira Wacker remains an important part of Austrian football history, known for its development of players and its long-standing presence in the national league system.

name: SC Wacker Wien
content:
SC Wacker Wien was an Austrian football club based in the Meidling district of Vienna, founded in the early 1900s (around 1906–1908). The club developed into one of the traditional Viennese teams and played its home matches at the Rosasgasse stadium near Schönbrunn. Wacker became a well-known side in Austrian football for its black-and-white colours and for being a strong competitor in the national league system over many decades.

During its peak years, especially in the 1940s and 1950s, Wacker Wien established itself as one of the leading teams in Austria. The club won the Austrian championship and the Austrian Cup in 1947, achieving the first and only double in its history. It also regularly finished near the top of the league, often as runners-up, and reached the Mitropa Cup final in 1951, which highlighted its international competitiveness.

In its later years, Wacker became less stable and experienced frequent relegations and promotions between divisions during the 1960s. Financial and sporting difficulties eventually led to a merger in 1971 with SK Admira Vienna, forming Admira Wacker, which became the legal successor of both clubs. After the merger, SC Wacker Wien ceased to exist as an independent club, but its legacy remained an important part of Austrian football history.

name: First Vienna FC
content:
First Vienna FC is one of the oldest football club in Austria, founded on 22 August 1894 in the Döbling district of Vienna. The club was established by a mix of British and Austrian gardeners working on the Rothschild estate, which also influenced its traditional blue and yellow colors. As a pioneer of football in Austria, First Vienna played an important role in the early development of the sport and participated in some of the first organized competitions in Central Europe, including winning the Challenge Cup in 1899 and 1900.

The club enjoyed its golden era between the late 1920s and 1940s, when it became one of the strongest teams in Austrian and Central European football. During this period, First Vienna won multiple Austrian championships and cups, as well as the prestigious Mitropa Cup in 1931. After the annexation of Austria in 1938, the club competed in the Gauliga system and achieved further success, including winning several league titles and the German Cup in 1943, while also reaching the German championship final in 1942.

Following World War II, the club added another Austrian championship in 1955 but gradually declined in the following decades, experiencing relegations and financial difficulties. Despite occasional returns to the top division and appearances in European competitions, First Vienna spent much of the late 20th and early 21st centuries in lower leagues. In recent years, however, the club has undergone a revival, achieving promotions and re-establishing itself as a competitive side while maintaining its historical importance in Austrian football.

name: SK Sturm Graz
content:
SK Sturm Graz is a professional football club based in Graz, Austria, founded in 1909. The team is known for its black and white colors and is one of the most successful clubs outside Vienna. Sturm plays its home matches at the Merkur Arena and has built a strong identity in Austrian football, with a loyal fanbase and a long-standing rivalry with local club Grazer AK.

The club experienced its greatest period of success in the late 1990s under coach Ivica Osim. During this era, Sturm Graz won back-to-back Austrian Bundesliga titles in 1998 and 1999 and became a regular participant in European competitions, including the UEFA Champions League. This period established the club as a major force in Austrian football and marked its rise to international recognition.

In the modern era, Sturm Graz has remained competitive at the top level, winning additional league titles in 2011, 2024, and 2025. The club is known for developing young talent and maintaining a strong sporting structure despite financial limitations compared to larger clubs. By consistently challenging dominant teams like Red Bull Salzburg, Sturm Graz continues to play a key role in Austrian football and has re-established itself as one of the country’s leading clubs.

name: Wiener Sport-Club
content:
Wiener Sport-Club is one of the oldest sports institutions in Austria, founded in 1883 in Vienna. Originally established as a multi-sport club, it included a wide range of disciplines such as cycling, fencing, athletics, and tennis before football became one of its most prominent sections. The club is based in the Dornbach district and plays its home matches at the historic Wiener Sport-Club Stadium, one of the oldest continuously used football grounds in the country.

The football team achieved its greatest success in the early and mid-20th century, winning three Austrian league titles in 1922, 1958, and 1959, as well as the Austrian Cup in 1923. One of the club’s most famous achievements came in 1958, when it defeated Juventus 7–0 in the European Cup, marking one of the most remarkable results in Austrian club football history. During this period, Wiener Sport-Club was also competitive in European competitions, reaching the quarter-finals of the European Cup.

In the later decades, the club faced financial difficulties, including bankruptcies in the 1990s, which led to its decline and relegation to lower divisions. In 2001, the football section separated as Wiener Sportklub before being reintegrated into the main club in 2017. Today, Wiener Sport-Club competes in the Austrian Regionalliga East (third tier) but remains an important historical club known for its traditions, loyal fanbase, and contribution to Austrian football.

name: Floridsdorfer AC
content:
Floridsdorfer AC is a professional football club based in the Floridsdorf district of Vienna, founded in 1904. It is one of the traditional clubs in Austrian football and plays in the Austrian 2. Liga. The team’s colors are blue and white, and it hosts its home matches at the FAC-Platz, a small stadium with a capacity of around 3,000 spectators.

The club experienced its greatest success during World War I, when it emerged as a strong competitor in Austrian football. After finishing as runners-up in 1916 and 1917, Floridsdorfer AC won its only Austrian championship in the 1917–18 season, even securing a historic double that year, although the cup victory was not officially recognized. This period remains the peak of the club’s sporting achievements.

In the decades that followed, Floridsdorfer AC struggled to maintain its top-level status, frequently moving between divisions and undergoing several mergers and restructurings. The club participated in the Gauliga during World War II after a merger and later spent long periods in lower leagues. In modern times, it has stabilized as a competitive team in the second tier, maintaining its historical identity while continuing to develop within Austrian football.

name: Wiener AF
content:
Wiener AF, also known as Wiener Assoziations-Footballclub (WAF), was an Austrian football club based in Vienna, founded in 1910 and dissolved in 2004. The club was created after most of the first-team players left Wiener AC to form a new team. WAF quickly became competitive, joining the top division and establishing itself as one of the stronger Viennese sides in the early years of Austrian football.

The club achieved its greatest success in the 1910s and early 1920s. It won the Austrian league title in the 1913–14 season and finished as runners-up in other campaigns, confirming its status among the top teams of that era. In 1921–22, Wiener AF also won the Austrian Cup, securing the only cup triumph in its history. These achievements marked the peak period of the club’s sporting success.

After its early success, Wiener AF began to decline, culminating in relegation from the top division in 1924. The club never returned to its former level and spent the following decades in lower leagues, undergoing various name changes and structural transformations. Despite eventually dissolving in 2004, its legacy remains tied to the formative years of Austrian football and the competitive Viennese football scene of the early 20th century.

name: Linzer ASK
content:
LASK (Linzer Athletik-Sport-Klub) is a professional football club based in Linz, Austria, and is the oldest club from the Upper Austria region. Founded in 1908, it plays in the Austrian Football Bundesliga and is known for its black and white colors. The team hosts its home matches at the modern Raiffeisen Arena, which has a capacity of over 19,000 spectators.

The club’s greatest achievement came in the 1964–65 season, when LASK became the first team from outside Vienna to win both the Austrian league title and the Austrian Cup, completing a historic double. This success marked a major breakthrough in Austrian football, which had previously been dominated by Vienna-based clubs. Throughout its history, LASK has also experienced periods of instability, including promotions and relegations between divisions.

In recent years, LASK has re-established itself as a competitive force in Austrian football. After financial difficulties and even a spell in the third division in the early 2010s, the club achieved promotion back to the Bundesliga in 2017 and has since regularly competed at a high level. It has also made notable appearances in European competitions, including strong runs in the UEFA Europa League, highlighting its modern resurgence and growing reputation.

name: FC Stahl Linz
content:
FC Stahl Linz (2013) is an Austrian football club based in Linz, Upper Austria, currently competing in the lower amateur divisions of the national league system. Although the modern club plays in the 2. Klasse Mitte, it represents a continuation of a much older football tradition in the city, with roots tracing back to a factory team formed in 1946.

The original club was founded as SV Eisen und Stahl Linz and later became known as SK VÖEST Linz, reflecting its connection to the state-owned steel company. It developed into a strong side and reached its peak in the 1973–74 season, when it won the Austrian championship. Over time, the club underwent several name changes, including FC Stahl Linz and FC Linz, but financial difficulties and the withdrawal of industrial support led to its dissolution in 1997 after a merger with local rivals LASK.

In 2013, FC Stahl Linz was re-established from the company football section of SK VÖEST, forming a new independent club. While competing at a much lower level than its predecessor, it is officially recognized as the successor to FC Linz and preserves its historical identity and legacy. Today, the club serves as a continuation of Linz’s industrial football heritage rather than a top-tier competitive force.

name: Grazer AK
content:
Grazer AK is a professional football club based in Graz, Austria, founded in 1902. The club, commonly known as GAK, is part of a larger multi-sport organization and plays its home matches at the Merkur Arena. Historically, it has been one of the most important clubs outside Vienna and is especially known for its fierce rivalry with local opponents Sturm Graz in the Graz derby.

The most successful period in the club’s history came between the late 1990s and mid-2000s. During this time, Grazer AK established itself as a major force in Austrian football, winning the Austrian Bundesliga title in 2003–04 and securing multiple Austrian Cups (1981, 2000, 2002, 2004). The 2003–04 season was particularly notable, as the club achieved a historic domestic double, marking the peak of its sporting success.

Despite its achievements, the club faced serious financial problems in the late 2000s, leading to administration, point deductions, and eventual bankruptcy. Grazer AK was dissolved in 2012 but was quickly re-founded by supporters as a phoenix club, starting from the lower divisions. After a steady rise through the league system, the club returned to professional football and eventually regained its place in the Austrian Bundesliga, marking a significant comeback in Austrian football.

name: Wiener AC
content:
Wiener AC (Wiener Athletiksport Club) is one of the oldest sports clubs in Austria, founded in 1897 in Vienna. While it became especially famous for its success in field hockey, its football section was also highly significant in the early development of Austrian football. In the early 1900s, Wiener AC won several important pre-league competitions such as the Challenge Cup and the Tagblatt Pokal, which were among the most prestigious tournaments before the establishment of a formal national championship.

The club reached its peak in football during the 1910s and 1930s. Wiener AC won the Austrian championship in the 1914–15 season and later achieved international recognition by reaching the final of the Mitropa Cup in 1931, one of the most important European club competitions of that era. The team also won multiple Austrian Cups (including 1930–31, 1937–38, and 1958–59) and competed in the Gauliga Ostmark during World War II, reflecting its continued presence at the top level.

In the post-war period, Wiener AC gradually declined and struggled to remain among Austria’s top clubs. A significant moment came in the 1970s, when it formed a joint team with FK Austria Wien (Austria/WAC Wien), with the results officially counted as part of Austria Wien’s history. Although the football section was later revived, it never returned to top-flight football and eventually stopped competing at a professional level, while the overall club continued to exist primarily through its other sports sections.

name: Hakoah Vienna
content:
Hakoah Vienna was a Jewish sports club founded in Vienna in 1909, whose name means “strength” in Hebrew. It quickly became one of the most important Jewish sports institutions in Europe, promoting physical culture among Jewish athletes at a time when many clubs excluded them. Hakoah was a multi-sport organization, but its football team gained particular fame, attracting players from across Europe and building a strong international following through tours in cities like London and New York.

The club reached its peak in the 1920s, becoming one of the strongest teams in Austrian football. Hakoah won the Austrian championship in the 1924–25 season, the first professional title in the country, and achieved international recognition with notable results, including victories over major foreign teams. Beyond football, the club was highly successful in other sports such as swimming, wrestling, and athletics, producing several Olympic athletes and becoming a symbol of Jewish sporting excellence.

However, the club’s success was abruptly ended in 1938 following the Anschluss, when Nazi authorities dissolved Hakoah, confiscated its facilities, and persecuted its members. Many athletes fled abroad, while others were killed during the Holocaust. After World War II, the club was re-established in 1945, but its football section ceased operations in 1949. Today, Hakoah continues as a sports organization, preserving its historical legacy and cultural significance.

name: SpC Rudolfshügel
content:
SpC Rudolfshügel was an Austrian football club based in the Favoriten district of Vienna, founded on 2 September 1902. It was named after the nearby hill “Rudolfshügel” and played in blue and white colours. The club became one of the early representatives of working-class football in Vienna and was among the founding participants of the first official Austrian football championship in the 1911–12 season. It played its home matches at the Rudolfshügel ground in Vienna.

During its peak years in the 1910s, Rudolfshügel established itself as a competitive top-flight team. The club’s greatest success came in 1919, when it finished as Austrian runner-up, marking the highest achievement in its history. It also reached the semi-finals of the Austrian Cup in 1915 and 1919 and spent multiple seasons competing at the highest level of Austrian football, often challenging stronger Viennese clubs such as Rapid and Floridsdorfer AC.

After its early success, the club gradually declined in the 1920s. Following relegation in 1923, Rudolfshügel briefly returned to the top division but struggled to remain competitive in the newly professionalized league system. Financial difficulties eventually led to suspension in 1927, and after several years in lower amateur competitions, the club was finally dissolved in 1934, ending its presence in Austrian football history.

name: Brigittenauer AC
content:
Brigittenauer AC was an Austrian football club based in Vienna, founded on 27 August 1925 through the merger of SC Donaustadt and FC Ostmark Wien. The club quickly rose through the league system and became a competitive side in Austrian football during the late 1920s. In its first top-flight season, it achieved a remarkable second-place finish in the Austrian championship, establishing itself as a strong newcomer in the national competition.

During the late 1920s and early 1930s, Brigittenauer AC experienced both highs and lows in performance. The club managed to win promotion back to the top division after relegation and later reached the final of the Austrian Cup in 1933, where it narrowly lost to Austria Wien. Despite moments of strong attacking football and high-scoring second-division campaigns, the team struggled to maintain consistency at the highest level.

After 1933, the club gradually declined due to sporting and financial problems. It withdrew from higher-level competition and dropped into lower regional leagues, eventually disappearing from professional football. In later decades, the club name survived through a series of mergers and reorganizations, but the original Brigittenauer AC ceased to exist as an independent top-level team, leaving behind a brief but notable legacy in early Austrian football history.

name: FC Wien
content:
FC Wien was an Austrian football club based in the Favoriten district of Vienna, founded in 1914 under the name SC Nicholson. The club was created as a tribute to the English football pioneer Mark D. Nicholson, and it initially developed in the lower and regional Viennese leagues before gradually rising through the system. In 1933, the club was renamed FC Wien and adopted red and white as its colours, becoming a stable participant in Austria’s top football competitions during the interwar period.

The club experienced its most successful sporting period during the 1930s and early 1940s. FC Wien competed in the highest leagues of Austrian football and achieved its best-ever result in the 1941–42 season, when it finished as runner-up in the Gauliga Ostmark. This era marked the peak of the club’s competitiveness, as it regularly played against Austria’s strongest teams and established itself as a solid top-flight side.

After World War II, FC Wien gradually declined in performance and stability. Although the club continued to compete in the Austrian league system for several decades, it struggled with financial problems and inconsistent results, eventually dropping out of the top division in the late 1950s. After further years in lower leagues, FC Wien was finally dissolved in 1973 due to economic difficulties, ending its professional history. The club was later re-founded in various forms, but none of these versions reached the level of the original team.

name: SV Ried
content:
SV Ried is a professional football club based in Ried im Innkreis, Upper Austria, founded on 5 May 1912. For most of its early history, the club played in regional Upper Austrian leagues and remained outside the national spotlight for decades. It was only in the early 1990s that SV Ried began rising through the Austrian football system, reaching the national divisions for the first time in 1991 and achieving promotion to the top tier in 1995.

The club’s first major breakthrough came in 1998, when SV Ried won the Austrian Cup by defeating Sturm Graz in the final. This victory marked the first major trophy in the club’s history and established it as a competitive Bundesliga side. During the following years, Ried experienced a mix of relegations and promotions, including a drop from the top division in 2003 and a quick return in 2005.

In the mid-2000s, SV Ried reached its strongest league performances. After returning to the Bundesliga, the club achieved its best-ever league result in 2006–07, finishing second and earning qualification for European competition. This period confirmed Ried as one of the strongest provincial clubs in Austria, capable of challenging more established teams despite its smaller budget and infrastructure.
stadium: BWT X Upper Austrian Arena, Cap. 7,680

name: 1. Simmeringer SC
content:
1. Simmeringer SC is an Austrian football club based in the Simmering district of Vienna, founded in 1901. It is one of the traditional Viennese clubs and was among the early participants in the development of organised Austrian football, competing in the first official national league seasons in the 1910s. The club played an important role in the early structure of Austrian football and quickly established itself as a competitive side in Vienna.

During the interwar period, Simmeringer SC experienced one of its strongest eras, particularly in the 1920s. The club achieved its best league result in 1925–26, finishing third in the Austrian championship, which marked the peak of its sporting success. Around this time, Simmering also played in a large and famous stadium, the Simmeringer Had, which at its peak held around 40,000 spectators and even hosted Austria national team matches.

After World War II, the club spent long periods alternating between divisions, including a stable run in the top flight from 1951 to 1964, where it was considered a solid Bundesliga side. However, financial difficulties and league reforms in the 1970s significantly affected the club’s position, eventually pushing it out of the top division. Despite later attempts to return to higher levels, Simmeringer SC gradually settled into lower-tier football, where it continues to compete today as a traditional Viennese club with a long historical legacy.

name: ASKÖ Pasching
content:
ASKÖ Pasching was an Austrian football club based in Pasching, Upper Austria, founded in 1946 as ATSV Pasching. For most of its early history, the club played in the lower regional leagues and remained largely amateur. It only began a rapid rise through the Austrian football pyramid in the 1990s, eventually reaching the national level and establishing itself as a competitive side in Upper Austrian football.

The club reached the Austrian Bundesliga in the early 2000s and competed there under various sponsor names, including SV PlusCity Pasching and FC Superfund. Its peak period came in the mid-2000s, when it achieved strong league finishes and notable results in European competitions. One of its most remarkable achievements was reaching the final of the UEFA Intertoto Cup in 2004, where it faced Schalke 04 after eliminating stronger opponents such as Werder Bremen.

Despite sporting success, the club faced financial instability and was dissolved in 2007 after its Bundesliga licence was transferred to SK Austria Kärnten. Following this, a successor club (FC Pasching) was formed, continuing football in the region but without direct legal continuity. Although ASKÖ Pasching no longer exists, it is remembered for its rapid rise from amateur football to European competition within a short period.

name: SV Mattersburg
content:
SV Mattersburg was an Austrian football club based in Mattersburg, Burgenland, founded in 1922. For most of its early history, the club competed in regional and lower national divisions, gradually building its status as a strong provincial team. It played its home matches at the Pappelstadion, which became one of the key football venues in Burgenland and symbolised the club’s rise from amateur football to the professional system.

The club’s major breakthrough came in the early 2000s, when it achieved promotion to the Austrian Bundesliga in 2003 after winning the second division. In its first top-flight years, SV Mattersburg quickly established itself as a stable Bundesliga side, attracting unusually large crowds for a small town club. Its best league performance came in the 2006–07 season, when it finished third in the Austrian championship, the highest ranking in its history.

Despite its sporting progress, SV Mattersburg collapsed in 2020 after the failure of its main financial backer, the Commerzialbank Mattersburg, which led to a major banking scandal. The club declared bankruptcy and was dissolved, ending its Bundesliga presence after nearly two decades. A successor club was later created to continue football activities at lower levels, but the original SV Mattersburg ceased to exist as a professional entity.

name: SV Grödig
content:
SV Grödig is an Austrian football club based in Grödig, in the state of Salzburg, founded on 20 March 1948. For most of its history, the club played in the regional leagues of Salzburg and remained outside the national spotlight. It gradually climbed through the Austrian football pyramid, especially from the early 2000s, when it achieved a series of promotions that brought it closer to professional football.

The club’s breakthrough came in the 2012–13 season, when SV Grödig won the Austrian First League (second division) and earned promotion to the Austrian Bundesliga for the first time in its history. Under coach Adi Hütter, the team quickly adapted to top-flight football and surprised many by performing strongly in its debut Bundesliga season.

Between 2013 and 2016, Grödig competed in the Austrian Bundesliga and even qualified for the UEFA Europa League in the 2014–15 season, marking the peak of its success. However, after the 2015–16 season, the club withdrew from professional top-level football due to financial and licensing decisions and dropped several tiers, returning to regional competition where it continues to play today.

name: SC Rheindorf Altach
content:
SC Rheindorf Altach is a professional football club based in Altach, in the Austrian state of Vorarlberg, founded on 26 December 1929. It originally began as the football section of the gymnastics club Turnerbund Altach and later went through several reorganizations, including temporary dissolutions during the 1930s and World War II period. The modern club structure was finally established in 1949 when it became independent as Sportclub Rheindorf Altach.

For most of its history, Altach played in regional and lower national leagues, gradually building its status as one of the strongest clubs in western Austria. The club achieved its first major breakthrough in the 2000s, reaching the second division and then winning promotion to the Austrian Bundesliga for the first time in 2006. Although it was relegated again in 2009, Altach returned to the top flight in 2014 after consistent strong performances in the second tier.

Since returning to the Bundesliga, SC Rheindorf Altach has established itself as a stable top-division club, occasionally achieving strong league finishes and qualifying for European competition, including the UEFA Europa League in the 2015–16 season. While it has not become a title contender, the club is respected for its organization, stability, and ability to compete despite relatively modest resources compared to Austria’s biggest teams.

name: Wolfsberger AC
content:
Wolfsberger AC is a professional football club based in Wolfsberg, Carinthia, Austria, founded in 1931. For most of its history, the club played in the lower divisions of Austrian football and remained a regional side without major national success. It is known as RZ Pellets WAC due to sponsorship reasons and plays its home matches at the Lavanttal-Arena, which has a capacity of around 7,300 spectators.

The club’s rise to prominence began in the 21st century, especially after a cooperation period with SK St. Andrä between 2007 and 2012, when the team competed under the name WAC/St. Andrä. In 2012, Wolfsberger won the Austrian First League (second division) and earned promotion to the Bundesliga for the first time in its history. In its debut top-flight season (2012–13), the club immediately established itself as a competitive side, finishing in the upper half of the table.

Since then, Wolfsberger AC has become a stable Bundesliga club and has also competed in European competitions, including the UEFA Europa League group stages in 2019–20. The club’s greatest achievement came in 2025, when it won the Austrian Cup, securing its first major national trophy. Today, WAC is considered one of the most consistent mid-table teams in Austria’s top division, known for its steady development and modern professional structure.

name: FC Kärnten
content:
FC Kärnten was an Austrian football club based in Klagenfurt, founded in 1920 under the name KSK Klagenfurt. Over the decades, the club underwent several name changes, most notably becoming SK Austria Klagenfurt before later evolving into FC Kärnten in 1999 after a merger with another regional team. The club represented the Carinthia region and played an important role in bringing top-level football to southern Austria.

The most successful period in the club’s history came in the early 2000s. In the 2000–01 season, FC Kärnten won the Austrian Cup and the Austrian Supercup, while also securing promotion to the Bundesliga after winning the second division. The team then competed in the top flight for several seasons and also participated in UEFA competitions, although it did not progress beyond the early rounds.

After this peak, the club entered a period of decline due to financial problems and structural changes in Austrian football. The creation of rival club SK Austria Kärnten in 2007, which took over key resources and the Bundesliga license, further weakened FC Kärnten. Facing increasing difficulties, the club went bankrupt and ceased operations in 2009, with its legacy continued by newer clubs in Klagenfurt.

name: Kremser SC
content:
Kremser SC is an Austrian football club based in the city of Krems, founded in 1919. The team traditionally plays in black and white and currently competes in the Austrian Regionalliga East, the third tier of the national football system. Throughout its history, the club has mainly represented regional football in Lower Austria, gradually developing from local competitions into a recognized name within Austrian football.

The club reached its peak in the 1980s and early 1990s. Its greatest achievement came in the 1987–88 season, when Kremser SC won the Austrian Cup in a surprising victory over FC Swarovski Tirol, marking the biggest success in its history. Shortly after, the team competed in the Austrian Bundesliga and even had famous players such as Mario Kempes in its squad, although it was relegated in the 1991–92 season.

After its top-flight period, Kremser SC experienced decline and spent most of the following decades in lower divisions. Despite financial and sporting challenges, the club has remained active and preserved its local importance, occasionally achieving strong results in regional competitions. Today, it continues to compete with ambitions of returning to higher levels while maintaining its legacy as one of the notable provincial clubs in Austrian football.

name: SV Stockerau
content:
SV Stockerau is an Austrian football club based in the town of Stockerau in Lower Austria, founded on 7 May 1907. The club has a long tradition in regional football and plays its home matches at the Stadion Alte Au. Throughout its history, it has mostly competed in the lower and regional tiers of the Austrian league system, representing one of the established provincial clubs outside the major football centers.

The club’s greatest success came in the early 1990s, when it achieved a historic victory in the 1990–91 Austrian Cup. In a remarkable final, second-division SV Stockerau defeated the much stronger Rapid Wien, securing the biggest achievement in its history. This triumph qualified the team for the 1991–92 UEFA Cup Winners’ Cup, where it faced Tottenham Hotspur, marking its only appearance in European competition.

After this golden period, SV Stockerau experienced a gradual decline, facing financial difficulties and dropping down the league system. Despite occasional promotions and restructurings, the club has remained in the lower divisions in recent decades. Today, it continues to compete at a regional level, maintaining its historical identity and legacy as a club remembered for one of the most surprising cup victories in Austrian football history.

name: SV Horn
content:
SV Horn is an Austrian football club based in the town of Horn in Lower Austria, founded on 21 October 1922. The team traditionally plays in blue and white, reflecting the city’s colors, and hosts its home matches at the Sparkasse Horn Arena, which has a capacity of around 3,500–4,000 spectators. For most of its history, the club competed in regional leagues and remained a modest provincial side.

The club’s rise began in the late 20th and early 21st century, when it achieved success in regional competitions and gradually climbed the Austrian football system. Its greatest achievement came in the 2007–08 season, when SV Horn won the Austrian Cup. However, this edition of the competition did not include top-tier clubs, which makes the victory somewhat unique in Austrian football history.

In more recent years, SV Horn has fluctuated between the second and third tiers of Austrian football. The club gained international attention in 2015 when Japanese star Keisuke Honda, through his management company, acquired a significant stake, aiming to develop the club further. Although the project was later scaled back, SV Horn continues to compete as a stable regional club, most recently playing in the Regionalliga East after relegation from the 2. Liga.

name: FC Juniors OÖ
content:
FC Juniors OÖ is an Austrian football club based in Pasching, Upper Austria, founded in 2007 as FC Pasching following the relocation of ASKÖ Pasching to Carinthia. The club represents a continuation of football in the region and plays in the lower tiers of the Austrian league system. It later rebranded to FC Juniors OÖ in 2017 and has maintained close links with LASK, often functioning as a development or partner team.

The club’s greatest achievement came in the 2012–13 season, when it won the Austrian Cup in one of the biggest surprises in the competition’s history. As a third-division team, FC Pasching defeated several top-flight clubs, including SK Rapid Wien and Red Bull Salzburg, before beating Austria Wien in the final. This made it the first third-tier club to win the cup and earned qualification for the UEFA Europa League play-offs.

In the following years, the club underwent structural changes, including a period of cooperation with LASK where it operated as a joint or reserve team in the Regionalliga. It later regained independent status and has continued to compete mainly in the second and third tiers of Austrian football. While it has not established itself as a top-flight club, FC Juniors OÖ remains notable for its unique cup triumph and its role in developing players within Austrian football.

name: SC Austria Lustenau
content:
SC Austria Lustenau is a professional football club based in Lustenau, in the western Austrian state of Vorarlberg, founded in 1914. It originated as the football section of a gymnastics association before becoming an independent club in 1936. The team plays in green and white and has long been one of the main representatives of football in the Vorarlberg region, competing mostly in the second tier of Austrian football.

During much of its history, Austria Lustenau played in regional leagues such as the Vorarlberger Liga, Arlbergliga, and later the Regionalliga West. The club experienced interruptions during World War II, when it was dissolved under Nazi rule, but was re-established after the war and continued competing at a regional level. Its first major national breakthrough came in the 1990s, when it won the Regionalliga West in 1994 and earned promotion to the second division, followed by winning the 2. Liga title in 1996–97.

Austria Lustenau has had several periods in the Austrian Bundesliga, though usually brief. It returned to the top flight in 2022 after winning the second division, marking its first Bundesliga appearance in over two decades. The club has also reached the Austrian Cup final twice (2011 and 2020), highlighting its ability to compete at a high level despite limited resources. Today, it is considered a stable club with a strong regional identity and a history of developing competitive teams in Austrian football.

name: SK Slovan HAC
content:
SK Slovan-Hütteldorfer AC is an Austrian football club based in the Hütteldorf district of Vienna, originally founded in 1902 as SK Slovan by the Czech minority in the city. The club emerged from a Czech sports association and became an important cultural and sporting institution for this community. In its early years, Slovan developed quickly and gained promotion to the top division in 1923, marking the beginning of its most competitive period in Austrian football.

During the 1920s and early 1930s, the club regularly competed in the top tier and achieved its best league result with a sixth-place finish in 1925–26. One of its most notable achievements came in 1924, when it reached the Austrian Cup final in a dramatic high-scoring match. However, Slovan often struggled to maintain its top-flight status and experienced multiple relegations and promotions during this era, eventually losing its place among the leading Austrian clubs.

The club faced significant challenges during and after World War II, including forced name changes under Nazi rule, when it temporarily became AC Sparta Wien. After the war, it briefly returned to the top division in 1950 but soon declined. In later decades, the club underwent several mergers, most notably with Hütteldorfer AC in 1976, forming its current identity. Since then, SK Slovan-Hütteldorfer AC has mainly competed in lower leagues, remaining a traditional Viennese club with strong historical roots rather than a top-level side.

name: SK Vorwärts Steyr
content:
SK Vorwärts Steyr is an Austrian football club based in the city of Steyr, founded in 1919, and is one of the oldest clubs in Upper Austria. The team traditionally plays in red and white and hosts its home matches at the Vorwärts Stadium. In its early years, the club quickly established itself at regional level, even winning the Upper Austrian league shortly after its foundation, at a time when a national championship had not yet been introduced.

The club experienced its most notable successes in the mid-20th century and later in the late 1980s and early 1990s. One of its greatest achievements was reaching the Austrian Cup final in 1949. Decades later, Vorwärts Steyr earned promotion to the Austrian Bundesliga in 1988 and remained there until 1996, achieving its best league results with mid-table finishes during the early 1990s. The club also participated in international competitions such as the Intertoto Cup in 1995, where it performed strongly in the group stage.

However, financial difficulties led to a major collapse at the end of the 1990s. The club went bankrupt in 2000 and was forced to restart from the lowest amateur divisions in 2001. Despite this setback, Vorwärts Steyr gradually climbed back through the league system and returned to the Austrian second tier in 2018. Today, the club continues to compete in the lower divisions, maintaining its identity as a traditional Austrian team with a loyal fanbase and a strong historical legacy.

name: 1. Wiener Neustädter SC
content:
1. Wiener Neustädter SC was an Austrian football club based in Wiener Neustadt, founded in 1908 and dissolved in 2010. The club was established by a group of Viennese students and adopted blue and white as its traditional colors. In its early decades, it became one of the strongest teams in Lower Austria, winning multiple regional championships in the 1920s and achieving its first major national success by winning the Austrian Amateur Championship in 1935–36.

After World War II, the club re-established itself and reached the top level of Austrian football. It gained promotion to the Staatsliga A in 1950 and again in 1959, competing against the country’s leading teams. One of the highlights of this period was the 1964–65 season, when Wiener Neustädter SC reached the Austrian Cup final. Although it lost the final, the club qualified for the UEFA Cup Winners’ Cup, marking its only appearance in European competition.

From the late 1960s onward, the club gradually declined, moving between the second and lower divisions and eventually dropping into regional and amateur leagues. Financial and structural challenges weakened the club over time, and in 2010 it was officially dissolved after its teams were taken over by FC Magna Wiener Neustadt. Despite its disappearance, the club remains an important part of Lower Austrian football history, particularly for its mid-20th-century achievements.

name: DSV Leoben
content:
DSV Leoben is an Austrian football club based in the Donawitz district of Leoben, founded on 1 February 1928 as Werkssportverein Donawitz. The club originated as a factory team connected to the Alpine Montan industry, which influenced both its identity and its nickname. Over time, it became one of the key football clubs in the Styria region, playing in green and white and hosting matches at the Donawitz Stadium.

The club experienced several periods in the Austrian top division, particularly under the name DSV Alpine during the 1970s and 1980s. Although it never became a major national power, it managed to spend around a decade in the Bundesliga and achieved respectable mid-table finishes. A significant moment came in 1992, when DSV Alpine merged with 1. FC Leoben to form the modern DSV Leoben, marking a new chapter in its history.

One of the club’s greatest achievements was reaching the Austrian Cup final in the 1994–95 season, where it lost to SK Rapid Wien. In later years, DSV Leoben faced financial difficulties, including administration in 2009, which led to relegation to lower leagues. Despite these setbacks, the club has remained active and recently achieved promotion back to the Austrian 2. Liga, continuing its role as a traditional regional club in Austrian football.

name: SV Feldkirchen
content:
SV Feldkirchen is an Austrian football club based in Feldkirchen in Kärnten (Carinthia), founded on 27 September 1948 through the merger of Arbeiterturnverein Feldkirchen (ATUS) and Blau-Weiß Feldkirchen. The club adopted green and white colours and developed as a typical regional team representing its local community. In its early decades, it mainly competed in Carinthian regional leagues and gradually built its identity within lower-tier Austrian football.

The club first reached the Kärntner Liga (Carinthian top regional division) in the 1958–59 season and experienced a long history of promotions and relegations between regional levels. One of its notable milestones came in the late 1970s and early 1980s, when SV Feldkirchen qualified several times for the Austrian Cup, even achieving a surprising win against Austria Klagenfurt in the 1981 edition. Over time, the club became known as a strong but inconsistent regional side, capable of occasional cup upsets but without sustained national-level presence.

In the modern era, SV Feldkirchen has continued to fluctuate between the Kärntner Liga and the Regionalliga, with several restructurings and financial challenges. The club has also formed partnerships with other local teams, such as SV Oberglan, to remain competitive. Despite occasional crises and even threats of dissolution in recent years, it has survived and remains active today, focusing on youth development and maintaining its role as a traditional regional club in Austrian football.

name: Wiener Neustadt SC
content:
Wiener Neustädter SC (2008) is a modern Austrian football club based in Wiener Neustadt, Lower Austria. It was founded on 19 May 2008 as FC Magna Wiener Neustadt after the takeover of SC Schwanenstadt’s league licence, with strong financial backing from businessman Frank Stronach and his company Magna International. The club quickly became a professional structure and used the Wiener Neustadt Arena as its home ground.

In its debut season (2008–09), the club achieved immediate success by winning the Austrian First League and earning promotion to the Bundesliga. Shortly after, it merged with the traditional club 1. Wiener Neustädter SC (1908), forming SC Wiener Neustadt while continuing to compete at the top level of Austrian football.

During the 2010s, the club spent several seasons in the Austrian Bundesliga, achieving mid-table stability but later suffering financial and sporting difficulties. In 2019 it lost its professional license and dropped to lower divisions. The club was later rebranded again as 1. Wiener Neustädter SC, continuing its existence in regional football while maintaining links to the original football tradition in Wiener Neustadt.

name: SKN St. Pölten
content:
SKN St. Pölten is an Austrian professional football club based in Sankt Pölten, the capital city of Lower Austria. It was founded in 2000 as Sportklub Niederösterreich St. Pölten after the collapse of its predecessor FCN St. Pölten (formerly VSE St. Pölten). The new club took over the football infrastructure, including the academy system and stadium, which allowed it to restart directly in the Austrian league system instead of the lowest amateur level.

In its first years, SKN St. Pölten climbed quickly through the divisions, winning consecutive promotions in the early 2000s and reaching the second tier within a short period. The club continued to develop steadily and became a stable side in the Austrian First League (now 2. Liga), frequently finishing in the upper half of the table. Its breakthrough at national level came in the 2013–14 season, when it reached the Austrian Cup final for the first time, later earning a place in the UEFA Europa League qualifiers despite losing the final to Red Bull Salzburg.

The club achieved promotion to the Austrian Bundesliga in 2016 after winning the second division and has since experienced several promotions and relegations between the top two tiers. It plays its home matches at the NV Arena, which has a capacity of around 8,000 spectators, and is known for its strong youth development system and modern structure. Today, SKN St. Pölten is considered a stable second-tier club with occasional Bundesliga appearances and a history of competing in European qualification rounds.

name: TSV Hartberg
content:
TSV Hartberg is an Austrian football club based in Hartberg, Styria, founded on 29 April 1946. For most of its history, the club played in lower regional leagues and remained a modest provincial side without national prominence. It gradually built its infrastructure and reputation in Styrian football, especially from the late 20th century onward, where it became a consistent competitor in regional competitions.

The club’s rise through the Austrian league system accelerated in the 2000s and 2010s. After several promotions and relegations between the third and second tiers, Hartberg achieved a major breakthrough in the 2017–18 season by finishing second in the 2. Liga, which earned the club its first-ever promotion to the Austrian Bundesliga. This marked a historic step, establishing Hartberg as a top-flight team for the first time.

Since reaching the Bundesliga, TSV Hartberg has established itself as a stable mid-table club capable of competing against Austria’s stronger sides. One of its most notable achievements came in the 2019–20 season, when it finished 5th in the league and qualified for the UEFA Europa League qualifying rounds. This period confirmed Hartberg’s transformation from a regional amateur club into a consistent professional Bundesliga participant.
